title: Fitting an open fireplace throat restrictor
subtitle: Reducing chimney heat loss when the fire is not lit
excerpt: An open fireplace with no restrictor loses 50-80 cubic metres of warm air per hour up the chimney even when the fire is not lit. A throat restrictor plate cuts this to near zero and takes under an hour to fit.
sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust open chimney and fireplace guidance. Historic England guidance on chimneys in traditional buildings for context on draught pressure in masonry flues.

content.text: A Victorian or Edwardian terraced house typically has several open fireplace openings. Even with the fire damper closed, the flue is open to the outside air and the stack effect draws warm room air upward continuously. A 225 mm x 225 mm chimney opening loses around 50-80 m3 of heated air per hour. This adds up across a heating season. A throat restrictor plate fitted above the opening reduces infiltration by around 90 percent and costs under thirty pounds to buy ready-made. The fitting process does not require tools beyond a tape measure.

A steel or aluminium plate with a central hole of around 100-150 mm diameter. The plate sits in the throat of the fireplace, resting on the sloping lintel above the opening. The central hole allows a small amount of ventilation to prevent condensation in the flue, and allows the fire to be lit without removing the plate. When the fire is lit, the draw through the central hole is enough to start the flue. This is the recommended solution for a fireplace used occasionally.

A polythene balloon on a nozzle, inflated with a foot pump to fill the flue cross-section above the opening. The balloon compresses against the flue walls and blocks air movement completely. Suitable for a fireplace that will not be used at all during the heating season. Must be fully deflated and removed before lighting the fire. A balloon left in place while a fire is lit will melt and produce toxic gases. Mark the balloon with a luggage tag warning.

A rigid board cut to fit the fireplace opening and held in place by friction or by magnets on the lintel. This blocks the opening rather than the throat. Less effective than a throat restrictor because it does not address the chimney volume above the opening, but easy to remove when the fire is wanted. The seal around the perimeter of the board is the most important factor in this approach.
Measuring and fitting a plate restrictor

Measure the throat opening. Put on eye protection and a dust mask. Stand to one side of the fireplace and measure the throat opening above the register plate: width and front-to-back dimension at the narrowest point. The throat is typically 200-350 mm wide and 80-150 mm front to back.

Select the matching plate size. Ready-made restrictor plates are available in 200, 225, 250, and 300 mm widths. Choose the size nearest to your throat width without going below it. If the plate is too narrow, cut a strip of mineral wool around the perimeter to fill the gap.
Slide the plate into the throat from below. The plate rests on the ledges each side of the throat or on the sloping fireback. Check it sits flat and does not rock.
Check the central hole is unobstructed. The hole allows moisture from the flue to disperse slowly. Do not block it.
Test the draught reduction. Stand in front of the closed fireplace and hold a hand over the opening. With the plate in place the air movement should be barely perceptible.

attrs.title: Using the fire with the plate in place

content.text: A plate restrictor with a central hole allows the fire to be lit without removal. The draw through the hole is enough to start the flue in most cases. Remove the plate if the fire smokes back at the start before the flue warms up.

Building Regulations require that a chimney flue serving a solid-fuel or gas appliance is not fully sealed. A completely blocked unused flue can develop condensation and allow condensate to soak through the chimney breast, staining walls. The central hole in a plate restrictor provides the minimum ventilation required. For a permanently decommissioned fireplace, a louvred register plate with a 50 mm ventilation slot is the correct long-term solution.

attrs.symptom: Chimney breast wall feels damp after fitting the restrictor
attrs.cause: Insufficient ventilation in the flue above the plate
attrs.fix: Increase the size of the central ventilation hole by drilling two or three additional 25 mm holes around the central one. The total ventilation area through the plate should be at least 5000 mm2 for a standard domestic flue.

attrs.symptom: Fire smokes into the room when first lit
attrs.cause: The cold flue needs time to warm up before draw establishes
attrs.fix: Hold a sheet of lit newspaper in the throat for one minute to warm the air column before lighting the main fire. Remove the restrictor plate if smoking persists for more than five minutes after the fire is established.
